--- Page 1 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
1
Enhanced Throughput and Seamless Handover
Solutions for Urban 5G-Vehicle C-Band Integrated
Satellite-Terrestrial Networks
Hung Nguyen-Kha , Graduate Student Member, IEEE, Vu Nguyen Ha , Senior Member, IEEE,
Eva Lagunas , Senior Member, IEEE, Symeon Chatzinotas , Fellow, IEEE,
and Joel Grotz , Senior Member, IEEE
AbstractвҖ”This paper investigates downlink transmission in
5G Integrated Satellite-Terrestrial Networks (ISTNs) supporting
automotive users (UEs) in urban environments, where base
stations (BSs) and Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites (LSats)
cooperate to serve moving UEs over shared C-band frequency
carriers. Urban settings, characterized by dense obstructions,
together with UE mobility, and the dynamic movement and
coverage of LSats pose signiп¬Ғcant challenges to user association
and resource allocation. To address these challenges, we formulate
a multi-objective optimization problem designed to improve
both throughput and seamless handover (HO). Particularly, the
formulated problem balances sum-rate (SR) maximization and
connection change (CC) minimization through a weighted trade-
off by jointly optimizing power allocation and BS-UE/LSat-
UE associations over a given time window. This is a mixed-
integer and non-convex problem which is inherently difп¬Ғcult to
solve. To solve this problem efп¬Ғciently, we propose an iterative
algorithm based on the Successive Convex Approximation (SCA)
technique. Furthermore, we introduce a practical prediction-
based algorithm capable of providing efп¬Ғcient solutions in real-
world implementations. Especially, the simulations use a realistic
3D map of London and UE routes obtained from the Google
Navigator application to ensure practical examination. Thanks
to these realistic data, the simulation results can show valuable
insights into the link budget assessment in urban areas due to
the impact of buildings on transmission links under the blockage,
reп¬Ӯection, and diffraction effects. Furthermore, the numerical
results demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed algorithms
in terms of SR and the CC-number compared to the greedy and
benchmark algorithms.
Index
TermsвҖ”LEO
Constellation,
Integrated
Satellite-
Terrestrial
Networks,
Seamless
Connectivity,
C-Band,
5G
Automotive, Resource Allocation.
I. INTRODUCTION
I
N recent years, wireless communication networks have
rapidly evolved to meet increasing trafп¬Ғc demands and
connectivity needs. Consequently, terrestrial networks (TNs)
have been swiftly deployed to accommodate this growth.
However, TNs often struggle to maintain the connectivity and
quality of service for a vast number of devices. Therefore,
the next-generation network aims to provide extensive and
seamless connectivity, addressing the high trafп¬Ғc demands
This work has been supported by the Luxembourg National Research Fund
(FNR) under the project INSTRUCT (IPBG19/14016225/INSTRUCT). The
preliminary result of this manuscript was presented in IEEE VTC FallвҖҷ24 [1].
H. Nguyen-Kha, V. N. Ha, E. Lagunas, and S. Chatzinotas are with
the Interdisciplinary
Centre for Security, Reliability
and Trust (SnT),
University of Luxembourg, 1855 Luxembourg Ville, Luxembourg. (e-
mail: khahung.nguyen@uni.lu; vu-nguyen.ha@uni.lu; eva.lagunas@uni.lu;
Symeon.Chatzinotas@uni.lu).
J. Grotz is with SES, Chateau de Betzdorf, Betzdorf 6815, Luxembourg
(e-mail: Joel.Grotz@ses.com).
and service requirements of new applications [2]вҖ“[4]. Es-
pecially, these requirements become more crucial in urban
environments that generally have high trafп¬Ғc and user demand.
A straightforward solution is the dense deployment of base
stations (BSs) to enhance network coverage and capacity.
However, this approach can be challenging and costly to
implement. Additionally, satellite communication (SatCom)
emerges as a promising solution to expand network coverage
and capacity [5]. Particularly, due to the ubiquitous and high
capacity, SatCom can provide complementary coverage, aid in
ofп¬Ӯoading for TNs, and jointly serve with TNs. This approach
is supported by the United States (US) Federal Communica-
tions Commission (FCC), which has proposed complementary
coverage for TNs from space segments [6]. Although satellite
coverage is typically seen as the solution for areas lacking
TN connectivity, using SatCom in urban environments, rather
than relying on dense base station (BS) deployment, presents
a cost-effective alternative. This approach not only enhances
coverage but also helps trafп¬Ғc ofп¬Ӯoading, addressing issues of
susceptibility to blockage and congestion events, respectively.
While SatCom was previously expensive, costs have now
decreased, especially for Low Earth Orbit satellites (LSats).
Hence, reasonable manufacturing and launching costs of LSats
led to many interests in developing LEO constellations such
as Starlink and OneWeb. Compared to geostationary and
medium-earth orbit satellites, LSats offer higher channel gain
and lower latency due to their lower altitude, making the
integration of satellite and TN systems more feasible [7].
Traditionally, TNs and SatCom Networks (SatNets) have
operated in distinct frequency bands (RFBs) due to spec-
trum regulation. However, to meet the growing demand for
connectivity, both networks have expanded their spectrum
utilization, resulting in the development of coexistence radio-
access systems. For instance, many countries have extended
the TN radio frequency (RF) spectrum into the millimeter-
wave frequency and C-band (3.4 вҲ’4.2 GHz). Concurrently,
signiп¬Ғcant efforts from academia and industry aim to lower the
RFBs of Non-Terrestrial Networks (NTNs), including SatNets,
from Frequency Range 2 (FR2) to Frequency Range 1 (FR1),
incorporating bands such as the S-band and L-band [8], which
can offer better penetration through precipitation and a longer
propagation distance compared to higher RFBs. Following this
trend, Mediatek has proposed scenarios in which the TN spec-
trum is shared with NTNs under complementary management
[9]. Notably, various coexistence scenarios between TNs and
NTNs have been investigated by 3GPP [10].
arXiv:2507.10308v1  [eess.SP]  14 Jul 2025


--- Page 2 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
2
In light of the evolving trend toward Integrated Satellite-
Terrestrial Networks (ISTNs), sharing the same RFBs between
TN and NTN systems is considered a promising solution
to enhance spectrum efп¬Ғciency and increase overall network
capacity. Based on the agreement between terrestrial and
satellite operators, BSs and LSats can work together in ISTNs
to provide Internet connectivity. This approach is further
supported by the FCC, which has proposed the co-primary
use of mobile and satellite services within the same RFBs
[6]. Furthermore, direct-to-device connectivity has attracted
signiп¬Ғcant attention and is supported by the European Space
Agency [11]. Hence, this approach offers the advantage of
improving seamless connectivity by allowing UEs to transit
smoothly between TN and NTN systems [6], [10]. Moreover,
the C-band has emerged as a critical RFB for future networks.
Historically reserved for Fixed Satellite Services (FSS), the
C-band is now attracting interest from TN operators due to
its wide coverage and high capacity. In the US, the FCC has
implemented a plan to repurpose 280 MHz of C-band spectrum
from FSS downlink (DL) to 5G, requiring satellite companies
to vacate this portion by December 2023 [12]. In Europe, the
Radio Spectrum Policy Group has identiп¬Ғed three key bands
for 5G deployment: 700 MHz for broad coverage, 3.4 GHz
for a balance between coverage and capacity, and 26 GHz for
high-speed data rates, with the 3.4 GHz C-band designated as
the primary band for implementation. Besides, 3GPP deп¬Ғned
speciп¬Ғc C-band frequencies (н‘ӣ77, н‘ӣ78, н‘ӣ79) for 5G-NR [13],
underscoring the C-bandвҖҷs pivotal role in the future of ISTNs.
Although LSats in ISTNs offer substantial beneп¬Ғts, such as
enhanced capacity, improved coverage, and seamless connec-
tivity, their inherent movement introduces signiп¬Ғcant dynamic
challenges. The extensive coverage provided by satellites also
introduces heterogeneity in ISTNs in terms of propagation
environments due to the diverse characteristics of the TN
areas (urban, suburban, rural) and even environmental differ-
ences within a single urban area [14]. This environmental
diversity necessitates accounting for the varying impact of
the environment within the ISTNвҖҷs coverage in system de-
sign. Additionally, UE mobility, particularly the movement
of vehicles in urban areas, further complicates the network
dynamics [1]. Although utilizing the same RFB in ISTNs, such
as the C-band for DL as per the current spectrum regulation,
can enhance spectrum efп¬Ғciency, increase system capacity,
and support seamless connectivity, it also introduces criti-
cal cross-interference issues between TN and NTN systems.
Consequently, system design must carefully address seamless
handovers, cross-system interference, and resource allocation,
factoring in the unique characteristics of urban environments
and realistic UE mobility to ensure these challenges are
adequately reп¬Ӯected in practical deployments.
A. Related Works
Recently, the analysis of interference in TN-NTN coexis-
tence and spectrum sharing systems has been investigated in
many works [10], [15]вҖ“[18]. In [10], the 3GPP has considered
various TN-NTN coexistence scenarios wherein TN and NTN
systems operate in adjacent bands in the S-band. Subsequently,
the throughput loss due to adjacent channel interference was
investigated. In [15], [16], the coexistence systems in C-band
were studied. Particularly, the authors in [15] analyzed the
impact of TNs on DL FSS systems wherein the aggregated
interference of 5G BSs at the ground FSS receiver was
investigated. Subsequently, the switch-off and power-back-off
schemes are proposed to reduce the interference from critical
BSs. The authors in [16] conducted the measurement of the
interference power caused by TN BSs to the FSS receiver.
Subsequently, the impact of various experimental deployment
scenarios of the tuner and п¬Ғlter on the interference has been
carried out. Additionally, in [17], [18], the performance anal-
ysis is studied based on the stochastic geometry. In particular,
the authors in [17] studied different coexistence scenarios
in the S-band, where the system performances in terms of
coverage and rate probability were analyzed. In [18], the
authors studied a system-level performance analysis for the
sharing systems, where the ergodic spectrum efп¬Ғciency of
LEO-SatNets is provided when the SatNet spectrum is shared
with TNs. However, in these works, the impact of complex
environments, such as in urban areas, is not considered.
Furthermore, these works focus on snap-shot model analysis
without considering the dynamics of systems.
Besides, the resource allocation in spectrum-sharing systems
has been studied in many works [19]вҖ“[24]. In [19]вҖ“[22],
the snap-shot-based spectrum-sharing scenarios for ISTNs
have been studied. In particular, the authors in [19] stud-
ied cooperative and competitive scenarios between TN and
SatNet systems, where the second-price auction mechanism
is designed to achieve equilibrium between channel sharing
from TNs and ofп¬Ӯoading ability from SatNets. In [20], the
authors studied the NOMA ISTNs where one satellite provides
the backhaul link for BSs and the access link for UEs in
the C-band. The system bandwidth (BW) is shared between
the backhaul and access links. Subsequently, the user asso-
ciation (UA), BW allocation, and power allocation (PA) are
optimized under the QoS and backhaul link constraints. In
[21], the author examined various TN-NTN spectrum-sharing
scenarios. Subsequently, the interference-aware and group-
sharing mechanisms are studied for NTN UL transmission in
the reverse pairing spectrum sharing scenario, which aims to
group and assign BSs and UEs to resource blocks. The authors
in [22] studied the rate splitting multiple access (RSMA)-
based spectrum sharing systems, wherein the SR maximization
problems in RSMA systems are considered for underlay and
overlay spectrum sharing scenarios. However, the snapshot-
based models in these works can not consider the dynamics
of networks, especially the LEO-SatNets. Furthermore, these
studies are limited to single-satellite scenarios.
Subsequently, the time-varying systems have been studied
in [23], [24]. In [23], authors proposed a dynamic spectrum-
sharing mechanism in which bandwidth allocation for each
network is determined based on its load, with coordination
managed by a spectrum management server. The authors in
[24] investigated beam management and scheduling in coex-
istence networks, focusing on LSat transmissions to satellite
cells. In this scenario, TNs and SatNets operate in separate
RFBs, with the TN band temporarily shared with SatNets.
To address this, the study proposed sequential solutions for


--- Page 3 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
3
handover, beam selection, and spectrum sharing. However, the
analysis was limited to the system level, without considering
BS/LSat-to-UE transmissions, which may pose challenges in
accounting for UE mobility. Furthermore, existing studies on
coexistence and spectrum-sharing systems predominantly rely
on statistical channel models, which limit their ability to
accurately capture environmental characteristics in the system
model. Although the TN-based UA/PA problems have been
extensively studied in literature such as [25], [26], the joint op-
timization of BS/LSat-UA/PA in co-primary spectrum-sharing
ISTNs, incorporating a realistic channel model based on an
actual three-dimensional (3D) urban map, remains largely
unexplored. This paper aims to address this research gap.
B. Research Contributions
In this work, we study a time-window (TW)-based UA
and PA mechanism for ISTNs in urban environments wherein
TNs and SatNets use the same RFB in the C-band. The main
contributions of this work can be summarized as follows.
вҖў We analyze time-varying systems and transmission mod-
els for ISTNs operating in the same RFB. Particularly,
the utilized channel and antenna models are especially
compatible with the ray-tracing (RT) mechanism and
the actual 3D maps, which can apply to various areas
thanks to the 3D map availability in the OpenStreetMap
resources [27]. Based on which we formulate a multi-
objective optimization problem that aims to maximize
the sum-rate (SR) while minimizing the number of con-
nection state changes (CCs), with joint optimization of
BS/LSat-UA and PA. This problem is categorized as
mixed-integer non-convex programming, which presents
challenges for direct solutions.
вҖў To address this problem, we propose two iterative algo-
rithms using the successive-convex-approximation (SCA)
method. The п¬Ғrst algorithm can provide the solution over
the entire TW but requires knowledge of the channel gain
for the entire TW, which can be challenging in practice.
For practical implementation, we further introduce the
second prediction-based iterative algorithm. Speciп¬Ғcally,
we develop an RT-based mechanism to sequentially pre-
dict the channel gain, enabling the solution of consecutive
sub-TW problems.
вҖў For practical simulations, we utilize a realistic 3D map
of London to assess the impact of the urban environ-
ment on ISTN channels and performance. Additionally,
UE mobility and network load are modeled based on
actual routes from Google Navigator and population data.
The simulation results provide valuable insights into the
impact of the urban environment with the blockage,
reп¬Ӯection, and diffraction effects on the time-varying link
budget. Moreover, the numerical results demonstrate the
superiority of our proposed algorithms over benchmark
schemes in solving the joint SR-CC optimization prob-
lem. Furthermore, the small gap in performance between
the two proposed algorithms highlights the effectiveness
of the prediction-based approach.
The preliminary result of this work were presented in [1]
where only the UA for SR maximization problem was studied.
Management System
Fig. 1: System model.
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section II
presents the system model and the problem formulation.
Section III and IV show the proposed algorithms, benchmarks,
and the complexity analysis. Section V and VI provide the sim-
ulation and numerical results and the conclusion, respectively.
II. SYSTEM MODEL
In this work, we study ISTNs as per Fig. 1, where LSats
and BSs jointly serve multiple automotive UEs. Speciп¬Ғcally,
we focus on urban environments where the impact of buildings
is considered, and the UEs represent moving vehicles on city
streets. According to the current spectrum regulation discussed
above with the coexistence and overlap of TN and NTN DL
transmission in the C-band, we study the DL operation of
ISTNs wherein we assume that TNs and NTNs operate in
the same RFB. Let N вүң{1, . . . , н‘Ғ}, M вүң{1, . . . , н‘Җ} and
K вүң{1, . . . , нҗҫ} denote the sets of BSs, LSats, and UEs,
respectively. In addition, let BSn, LSatm and UEk be the
н‘ӣвҲ’th BS, н‘ҡвҲ’th LSat, and н‘ҳвҲ’th UE, respectively. To account
for other UEs in the system, we also assume the presence
of terrestrial UEs (TUEs) and satellite UEs (SUEs), served
by BSs and LSats, respectively. Additionally, we employ a
window-based model consisting of н‘ҒTS time slots (TSs),
where the duration of each TS is н‘ҮS. During this TW, each
automotive vehicle UE is moving in its predetermined route.
Subsequently, based on the satellite orbit and UE movement,
we assume that the time alignment at LSats is perfect. Assume
that each LSat operates in a quasi-п¬Ғxed-beam mode and LSats
point their beam towards the designated serving area [28].
Additionally, one assumes that BSs and satellite gateways are
connected to a management system, wherein each node can
exchange data with the core network and the center network
controller (CNC) can manage the system resource.
A. Tx-UE Channel Model
This subsection presents the channel model of BS-UE and
LSat-UE links which follows the coexistence of terrestrial
and satellite system scenarios described in 3GPP TR 38863
[10]. In particular, for simpliп¬Ғcation, let в„Һн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўand н‘”н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўbe
the effective channel gain coefп¬Ғcients of the BSн‘ӣвҲ’UEн‘ҳand
LSatн‘ҡвҲ’UEн‘ҳlinks at TS н‘Ўwhich consist of the path-loss and
the radiation beam pattern created by the terminal antennas,
respectively. Subsequently, the channel gain is calculated by
the inverse of the corresponding path loss (PL).
1) Path-loss Model: Based on the multi-path channel model
[14], [29], let Tx represent either BS or LSat, the equivalent
PL of Txн‘ҘвҲ’UEн‘ҳ, н‘ҘвҲҲ{н‘ҡ, н‘ӣ} link at TS н‘Ўcan be modeled as

For LSat-UE links, due to the very high altitude compared
to the distance between UEs and scatterers on the ground,
one assumes that the Doppler shifts of all rays are identical,
i.e., н‘“D,н‘Ў

satellite orbit and UE position knowledge, one assumes that the
Doppler shift can be compensated at the LEO satellite payload

departure at Txн‘Ҙcorresponding to ray н‘–at TS н‘Ў. Especially,
ray н‘–, н‘–вүҘ1, is the LoS ray or the ray reaching UE by reп¬Ӯection
or diffraction on obstacles in the environment. In case existing
obstacles, ray н‘–= 0 is the ray propagating through the wall to
reach the UE, the channel gain loss of this ray can be modeled
as twice of the wall-loss as [31]

2) Antenna Model: In this work, we assume that the vehicle
UE is equipped with a low-cost patch antenna on the rooftop
with no beamforming capabilities, i.e. pointing to the Zenith
(as shown in Fig. 2). Additionally, its radiation pattern can be
modeled by employing a cosine pattern as
ВҜнҗәveh(н‘Һн‘§, н‘’н‘ҷ) = cos2 ВҜн‘ҡ(н‘Һн‘§) cos2 ВҜн‘ӣ(н‘’н‘ҷ),

where ВҜн‘ҡand ВҜн‘ӣare the order of the. az and el are the azimuth
and elevation angles in the OxвҖІyвҖІzвҖІ coordinate system in which
its OyвҖІzвҖІ plane is mounted on the vehicleвҖҷs rooftop. Besides,
azimuth and elevation angles нң‘and нңғin нҗәr(нң‘, нңғ) in (1) are
measured in the Oxyz coordinate system where its Oxy plane
mounted on the vehicleвҖҷs rooftop. Hence, it is noted that
angles az and el can be computed from angles нң‘and нңғby
the coordinate rotation operation. For instance, the directivity
gain pattern of the patch antenna in the Oxyz and OxвҖІyвҖІzвҖІ
coordinate systems is illustrated in Fig. 2. Regarding the LSatвҖҷs
antenna, its antenna gain pattern is modeled as [28].

B. BS/LSat-UE Association Model
To indicate the BS-UA, we п¬Ғrst introduce binary variable
нң¶вүң[нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) as
нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў=
(
1,
if UEн‘ҳconnects to BSн‘ӣat TS н‘Ў,
0,
otherwise.
(6)
In addition, one assumes that each UE can connect to at most
one BS at each TS, which is ensured as
(нҗ¶1) :
Г•

(7)
Regarding the available BS-UA at each TS, assuming that BSн‘ӣ
can serve at most нң“B

limitation at each BS can be cast by

н‘ӣ,н‘Ўis the number of TUEs1 served by BSн‘ӣat TS н‘Ў.
Regarding the LSat-UA, one can see that UEs can connect to
the LSat if it is in the п¬Ғeld of view (FoV) of UEs. Let Оҳн‘Ў=
{вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘ҳ)|нңғн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүҘЛңнңғ} be the set of LSat-UE pairs satisfying
the FoV criteria above at TS н‘Ў, wherein нңғн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўis the elevation
angle at UEн‘ҳtoward LSatн‘ҡat TS н‘Ўand Лңнңғis the minimum
elevation angle requirement. Next, we introduce the LSat-UA
variable нң·вүң[нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) corresponding to TS н‘Ўas
нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў=
(
1, if (н‘ҡ, н‘ҳ)вҲҲОҳн‘Ўand UEн‘ҳconnects to LSatн‘ҡ,
0,
otherwise.
(9)
Assuming that each UE can connect to at most one LSat at
each TS while each LSatн‘ҡcan serve at most нң“S
н‘ҡUEs at each
TS. These yield the following constraints
(нҗ¶3) :
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҡнӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүӨ1,вҲҖ(н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),
(10)
(нҗ¶4) :
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҳнӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүӨнң“S
н‘ҡвҲ’нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў,вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘Ў),
(11)
where нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ўis the number of SUEs2 served by LSatн‘ҡat TS н‘Ў.
1The presence of TUEs is used to emulate the congestion caused by other
UEs at BSs.
2The presence of SUEs is used to emulate the congestion caused by other
UEs at LSats.


--- Page 5 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
5
Regarding UE experience, to ensure the quality of experi-
ence (QoE) in terms of seamless connectivity and to guarantee
that all UEs will be served, we assume that each UE has at
least one connection at each TS, which is ensured as
(нҗ¶5) :
Г•
вҲҖн‘ӣнӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў+
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҡнӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүҘ1,вҲҖ(н‘ҳ, н‘Ў).
(12)
Additionally, due to system dynamics, UEs must adapt
their association with BSs and LSats to enhance throughput
performance. Particularly, UEs may carry out the HO, establish
an additional connection, or remove the existing connection.
Based on the UA decision, the connection state transition
procedure can be executed accordingly to the system standard
[33]. Thanks to the UA, the number of connection state
changes (CCs) is quantiп¬Ғed by evaluating the difference in
UA decisions between consecutive TSs as follows
н‘“CC(нң¶, нң·) =
Г•
вҲҖн‘Ў>1
 Г•
вҲҖ(н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ)
|нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’1|+
Г•
вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ)
|нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’1|

.
(13)
C. Transmission Model
This subsection describes the transmission from BSs and
LSats to UEs. Since TNs and NTNs operate in the same RFB,
the transmission involves intra-system interference (IaSI) and
inter-system interference (ISI). Depending on the standard in
each system, based on the employed resource allocation and
multiple access technique, we assume that BSs are coordinated
to mitigate the TN IaSI such as in [25], [26], [34] and LSats are
coordinated for that in NTNs such as in [35], [36]. However,
due to the long distance between LSats and ground segments,
TNs and NTNs are not coordinated to mitigate ISI. Hence, for
simpliп¬Ғcation, we assume that IaSI is negligible and only ISI
between TNs and NTNs is considered. Consequently, if UEн‘ҳ
connects to BSн‘ӣ, the corresponding signal-to-interference-
plus-noise-ratio (SINR) can be expressed as
нӣҫB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S) =
нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўв„Һн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў
Оһн‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң·, н‘·S) + нңҺ2
н‘ҳ
,
(14)
where нңҺ2
н‘ҳis the additive white Gaussian noise power and
Оһн‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң·, н‘·S) = ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ҡ(ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ҳвҖІ нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳвҖІ,н‘Ўн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳвҖІ,н‘Ў+ нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘ЎВҜн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў)н‘”н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўis ISI
caused by LSats at UEн‘ҳ. Speciп¬Ғcally, the п¬Ғrst summation and
the second term in the brackets are the total transmit power
of LSatн‘ҡto нҗҫUEs and its own нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘ЎSUEs, respectively.
Particularly, н‘·B вүң[н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў), н‘·S вүң[н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў), н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў
and н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўare the transmit powers of BSн‘ӣand LSatн‘ҡto UEн‘ҳ
at TS н‘Ў, respectively. ВҜн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ўis the transmit power of LSatн‘ҡto
each of its нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘ЎSUEs which is uniformly allocated as
ВҜн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў= н‘ғS,max
н‘ҡ
/min{нң“S
н‘ҡ, нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў+ нҗҫ},вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘Ў),
(15)
wherein н‘ғS,max
н‘ҡ
is the maximum power budget of LSatн‘ҡ
and min{нң“S
н‘ҡ, нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў+ нҗҫ} indicates the maximum number of
UEs which can connect to LSatн‘ҡat TS н‘Ў. Subsequently, the
throughput from BSн‘ӣto UEн‘ҳat TS н‘Ўcan be computed as
н‘…B
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S) = log

1 + нӣҫB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S)

.
(16)
Regarding the LSat-UE connection, if UEн‘ҳconnects to
LSatн‘ҡ, the corresponding SINR can be expressed as
нӣҫS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S) =
нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўн‘”н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў
ОҰн‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, н‘·B) + нңҺ2
н‘ҳ
,
(17)
where ОҰн‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, н‘·B) = ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ӣ
ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ҳвҖІ нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳвҖІ,н‘Ўн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳвҖІ,н‘Ў+ нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘ЎВҜн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў

в„Һн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў
is the ISI caused by BSs, the п¬Ғrst summation and the second
term inside brackets are the total transmit power of BSн‘ӣto нҗҫ
UEs and its own нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘ЎTUEs, respectively. Particularly, ВҜн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ўis
the transmit power from BSн‘ӣto each of its нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘ЎTUEs which
is uniformly allocated as
ВҜн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў= н‘ғB,max
н‘ӣ
/min{нң“B
н‘ӣ, нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў+ нҗҫ},вҲҖ(н‘ӣ, н‘Ў),
(18)
н‘ғB,max
н‘ӣ
is
the
maximum
power
budget
of
BSн‘ӣ
and
min{нң“B
н‘ӣ, нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў+ нҗҫ} is the maximum number of UEs which
can connect to BSн‘ӣat TS н‘Ў. Subsequently, the transmission
throughput from LSatн‘ҡto UEн‘ҳcan be formulated as
н‘…S
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S) = log

1 + нӣҫS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S)

.
(19)
Hence, the aggregated transmission throughput of UEн‘ҳat TS
н‘Ўcan be expressed as
н‘…н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B,н‘·S) =
Г•
вҲҖн‘ӣ
н‘…B
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B,н‘·S)+
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҡ
н‘…S
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B,н‘·S).
(20)
Regarding the QoS of UEs, the rate threshold constraint
should be considered. However, ensuring this constraint at
each TS may be infeasible since the transmission links can
be blocked by buildings in urban environments at certain TSs.
Hence, one can guarantee the QoS of UEs by considering the
minimum average rate constraint at consecutive periods as
(нҗ¶6) :
1
н‘ҒQoS
TS
Г•
вҲҖн‘ЎвҲҲTн‘һ
н‘…н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S) вүҘВҜн‘…н‘ҳ,вҲҖн‘ҳ, н‘һ> 0,
wherein Tн‘һ= {(н‘һвҲ’1)н‘ҒQoS
TS
+ 1, . . . , н‘һн‘ҒQoS
TS } is the set of TSs
in period н‘һ, each period consists of н‘ҒQoS
TS
TSs, and ВҜн‘…н‘ҳis the
average rate threshold of UEн‘ҳat each period.
D. Problem Formulation
In this subsection, we formulate a multi-objective optimiza-
tion problem aiming to maximize the average system SR and
minimize the average number of CCs per TS, wherein the UA
and PA are jointly optimized.
Assuming that the deployment of BSs and LSats is sufп¬Ғ-
ciently dense, UEs may have multiple connection options at
each TS. However, due to the limitation of radio resources,
BSs and LSats can overload at certain times depending on
the number of connected devices. In addition, the system is
highly dynamic due to UE mobility, LSat movement, and
the complexity of urban environments, leading to frequent
variations in link availability and quality over time.
Therefore, to ensure connectivity under such dynamics,
UEs must appropriately adapt their associations with BSs and
LSats through HO, establishing new connections, or releasing
existing ones. Concurrently, due to the BS/LSatвҖҷs transmit
power limit and the ISI impact, an appropriate PA is essential.
Besides, each connection state transition incurs signaling
overhead between UEs and the corresponding BS/LSat. Fur-
thermore, unnecessary state transitions may arise, which are
undesirable and should be minimized. Therefore, in addition
to enhancing throughput and enabling seamless HO, the opti-
mization goal must also aim to minimize the number of CCs.
Particularly, the optimization is mathematically formulated as
(P0)
max
нң¶,нң·,н‘·B,н‘·S
нңҢ
н‘ҒTS
Г•
вҲҖ(н‘ҳ,н‘Ў)
н‘…н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(нң¶, нң·, н‘·B, н‘·S) вҲ’1 вҲ’нңҢ
н‘ҒTS н‘“CC(нң¶, нң·)
s.t.
constraints (нҗ¶1) вҲ’(нҗ¶6),
(нҗ¶0) :
нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў, нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҲ{0, 1},вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘ӣ, н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),


--- Page 6 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
6
Original
problem

o ve or
e ne

binary
variables

Proposition 3
Fig. 3: Flowchart of the full-time-window algorithm
(нҗ¶7) :
нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘ЎВҜн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў+
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҳнӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүӨн‘ғB,max
н‘ӣ
,вҲҖ(н‘ӣ, н‘Ў),
(нҗ¶8) :
нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘ЎВҜн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў+
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҳнӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ўн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүӨн‘ғS,max
н‘ҡ
,вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘Ў),
wherein нңҢвҲҲ(0, 1) is the multi-objective factor, a higher
нңҢleads to higher priority of SR maximization. In addition,
constraints (нҗ¶7) and (нҗ¶8) ensure that the transmit power does
not exceed the BS and LSat power budgets, respectively. The
outcome of solving this problem can provide solutions for UA
and PA over time, helping to improve system performance.
However, due to the non-convexity of the rate functions, and
the coupling between binary and continuous variables, this
problem belongs to mixed-integer non-linear programming
which is difп¬Ғcult to solve directly.
III. PROPOSED SOLUTIONS
This section proposed two algorithms for the full time
window and for the prediction time window. The developing
procedure of the п¬Ғrst algorithm is summarized in Fig. 3.
A. Problem Transformation
Analyzing the (P0) structure and its optimization variables,
we obtain the following relationships, each TS н‘Ў
вҖў If UEн‘ҳconnects to BSн‘ӣ, н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў> 0, and н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў= 0 if
UEн‘ҳdoes not connect to BSн‘ӣ.
вҖў If UEн‘ҳconnects to LSatн‘ҡ, н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў> 0, and н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў= 0 if
UEн‘ҳis not served by LSatн‘ҡ.
Based on these relations, the binary variable can be represented
by the в„“0вҲ’norm components of continuous ones as
нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў= вҲҘн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҘ0,вҲҖ(н‘ӣ, н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),
(21a)
нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў= вҲҘн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҘ0,вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘ҳ, н‘Ў).
(21b)
One notes that representations in (21) are valid for вҲҖн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүҘ0
and вҲҖн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүҘ0. Additionally, due to the FoV criteria for
LSat-UA in (9), the LSatвҖҷs power control elements are set as
н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў= 0, вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘ҳ) вҲүОҳн‘Ў. By employing the relations in (21),
the binary terms in rate and SINR functions can be omitted.
Subsequently, problem P0 can be rewritten as
(P1) :
max
н‘·B,н‘·S,нқҖB,нқҖS
нңҢ
н‘ҒTS
ЛңнңҶвҲ’1 вҲ’нңҢ
н‘ҒTS
ВҜн‘“CC(н‘·B, н‘·S)
s.t.
( ВҜнҗ¶1) :

(нҗ¶10) :
нңҶS

н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў(н‘·B, н‘·S),
вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),
where ВҜн‘“CC(н‘·B, н‘·S) is rewritten from н‘“CC(нң¶, нң·) as
ВҜн‘“CC(н‘·B, н‘·S) =
Г•
вҲҖн‘Ў>1
(

вҲҖ(н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ)

Г•
вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ)

In addition, ЛңнңҶ= ГҚ
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎнңҶB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў+ ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎнңҶS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў, and нқҖB
вүң
[нңҶB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) and нқҖS вүң[нңҶS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) are the slack variables
which are considered the lower bounds of the rate functions as
in constraints (нҗ¶9) and (нҗ¶10), respectively. It can be seen that
problem (P1) is still difп¬Ғcult to solve due to the nonconvex
rate constraint (нҗ¶9) вҲ’(нҗ¶10) and the sparse в„“0вҲ’norm terms.
The sparsity in в„“0вҲ’norm terms and the non-convexity in rate
constraints will be addressed by appropriate approaches in the
following sub-sections.
B. Proposed Iterative Solution
1) Approximate в„“0-norm components: To address the spar-
sity in problem (P1), the в„“0-norm components can be approx-
imated by a concave function as
вҲҘн‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҘ0 вүҲн‘“apx(н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў),вҲҖ(н‘ӣ, н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),
(23a)
вҲҘн‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҘ0 вүҲн‘“apx(н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў),вҲҖ(н‘ҡ, н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),
(23b)
in which н‘“apx(н‘Ҙ) вүң1вҲ’н‘’вҲ’нңҒн‘Ҙ, н‘ҘвүҘ0 is the approximate concave
function, нңҒis a positive number used to control the smoothness
of the approximated function [34]. Thanks to (23), ( ВҜнҗ¶5) can
be transformed into the convex constraint as
( Лңнҗ¶5) :
Г•
вҲҖн‘ӣ
н‘“apx(н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) +
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҡ
н‘“apx(н‘ғS
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) вүҘ1,вҲҖ(н‘ҳ, н‘Ў).
Proposition 1. Constraints ( ВҜнҗ¶1) вҲ’( ВҜнҗ¶4) can be approximated
respectively at iteration (н‘–+ 1) as
( Лңнҗ¶1) :
Г•
вҲҖн‘ӣ
Лңн‘“(н‘–)
apx(н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) вүӨ1,вҲҖ(н‘ҳ, н‘Ў),
( Лңнҗ¶2) :
Г•
вҲҖн‘ҳ
Лңн‘“(н‘–)
apx(н‘ғB
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) вүӨнң“B
н‘ӣвҲ’нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў,вҲҖ(н‘ӣ, н‘Ў),
( Лңнҗ¶3) :

вҲҖн‘ҡ
Лңн‘“(н‘–)
apx(н‘ғS

н‘Ҙ(н‘–) is the feasible point at iteration н‘–.
Proof: Thanks to (23), the в„“0-norm components in con-
straints ( ВҜнҗ¶1) вҲ’( ВҜнҗ¶4) can be approximated by н‘“apx(В·). How-
ever, the approximated constraints are nonconvex due to the
concavity of н‘“apx(В·). Subsequently, by utilizing the п¬Ғrst-order

approximation, an upper bound of н‘“apx(н‘Ҙ) around н‘Ҙ(н‘–) is
obtained as

Thanks to Propositions 1, 2, and 3, problem (P1) can be
transformed into a successive convex problem at iteration н‘–as
(P2) :

for problem (P0). Besides, the UA solution is retrieved as

wherein нң–is a small positive number. The proposed iterative
algorithm for full time-window-based (FTW), so called FTW
algorithm, is summarized in Algorithm 1. The convergence of
this algorithm is discussed in the following proposition.
Proposition 4. Algorithm 1 converges to a local optimal
solution of problem (P0).
Proof: First, one needs to prove the convergence of Algo-
rithm 1. Let О© вүң(н‘·B, н‘·S, нқҖB, нқҖS, нқҒB, нқҒS, н’Ӯ, н’ғ) be the variable
tuple, and О©(н‘–), О©вҳ…(н‘–), and F (н‘–) be the feasible point, optimal
Algorithm 1 FTW ALGORITHM FOR SOLVING PROBLEM (P0)
1: Set н‘–= 0 and generate an initial point (н‘·B,(0), н‘·S,(0), нқҒB,(0), нқҒS,(0) ) by
solving problem (27).
2: repeat
3:
Solve problem (P2) to obtain (н‘·B,вҳ…, н‘·S,вҳ…, нқҒB,вҳ…, нқҒS,вҳ…).
4:
Update (н‘·B,(н‘–),н‘·S,(н‘–), нқҒB,(н‘–), нқҒS,(н‘–) ):= (н‘·B,вҳ…, н‘·S,вҳ…, нқҒB,вҳ…, нқҒS,вҳ…).
5:
Set н‘–= н‘–+ 1.
6: until Convergence
7: Recovery UA solutions нң¶and нң·by (26).
8: Output: The solution for problem (P0) (нң¶вҳ…, нң·вҳ…, н‘·B,вҳ…, н‘·S,вҳ…).

solution, and feasible set of (P2) at iteration н‘–. In addition,
let нҗ№(н‘–) (О©) denote the objective value of (P2) with solution
О© at iteration н‘–and feasible point О©(н‘–). Since (P2) is derived
by employing the SCA technique, it satisп¬Ғes the properties of
SCA-based problem [37]. In particular, we have О©вҳ…(н‘–) вҲҲF (н‘–)вҲ©
F (н‘–+1). This leads to нҗ№(н‘–+1) (О©вҳ…(н‘–+1)) вүҘнҗ№(н‘–) (О©вҳ…(н‘–)). Hence,
the objective value sequence is non-decreasing. Additionally,
the transmit power is bounded by constraints ( ВҜнҗ¶7) вҲ’( ВҜнҗ¶8),
which prove the convergence of Algorithm 1. Subsequently,
with (нң¶, нң·) recovered from (н‘·B, н‘·S) by (26), one can see that
the feasible set of (P2) is subset of that of (P0). Therefore,
Algorithm 1 converges to a local solution of (P0).

requires

constraints ( Лңнҗ¶5), ( Лңнҗ¶6) [38], and solve the following problem

variables. An initial point can be found by solving problem
(27) until convergence.
C. Prediction-based Solution
The implementation of the proposed FTW algorithm above
requires the channel gain information of all TSs in the consid-
ered time window which is difп¬Ғcult to achieve in practice if the
number of TSs is sufп¬Ғciently large. Hence, in this sub-section,
we further propose a prediction-based solution. The overall
approach of this solution is described in Fig. 4. Particularly,


--- Page 8 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
8
BSs
LSats
CNC
UEs
Fig. 5: Information exchange in the prediction-based algorithm
the entire TW is divided into multiple sub-TWs with the size of
н‘Ғpred
TS , yielding н‘Ғpred
TW = вҢҲн‘ҒTS/н‘Ғpred
TS вҢүsub-TWs. Subsequently,
the channel gain information of each sub-TW is predicted
based on the UEвҖҷs information in the previous one. For
convenience, let T pred
нң…
= {(нң…вҲ’1)н‘Ғpred
TS
+ 1, . . . , нң…н‘Ғpred
TS } be
the set of TSs in sub-TW нң….
Supposing that the satellite orbit information, BSвҖҷs position,
and 3D map knowledge are available, one can further assume
that the channel gain of LSat/BS-UE links can be identiп¬Ғed
by employing the RT mechanism for the given UEвҖҷs position.
However, to obtain the channel gain information in a sub-
TW while avoiding the frequent update of UEвҖҷs position at
each TS, one necessitates predicting the UE position based on
UEвҖҷs information in the past. It is worth noting that UE is the
vehicle, for the given UE route which can be obtained by the
navigator application, e.g., Google Navigator, the UE position
can be predicted as follows. Regarding the UE mobility, it can
be seen that the changing of the UEвҖҷs velocity and trajectory in
each sub-TW is negligible for a sufп¬Ғciently small window size.
Hence, let н‘Јн‘ҳ,н‘Ўand ЛҶн‘Јн‘ҳ,н‘Ўbe the actual and predicted velocities
of UEн‘ҳat TS н‘Ў, UEн‘ҳвҖҷs velocity in sub-TW нң…is predicted as
ЛҶн‘Јн‘ҳ,н‘Ў=
1
н‘Ғpred
TS
Г•
вҲҖн‘ЎвҖІ вҲҲTpred
нң…вҲ’1 н‘Јн‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҖІ,
вҲҖн‘ЎвҲҲT pred
нң…
,вҲҖн‘ҳ.
(28)
Subsequently, let н‘‘н‘ҳ,н‘Ўbe the distance from the starting point to
the UEн‘ҳвҖҷs position at TS н‘Ўalong the given route, the predicted
distance ЛҶн‘‘н‘ҳ,н‘Ўin sub-TW нң…вҲ’th can be identiп¬Ғed as
ЛҶн‘‘н‘ҳ,н‘Ў= н‘‘н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҖІ + н‘ҮS ЛҶн‘Јн‘ҳ,н‘Ў,
вҲҖн‘ЎвҲҲT pred
нң…
,
(29)
where н‘ЎвҖІ = (нң…вҲ’1)н‘Ғpred
TS
is the TS before sub-TW нң…. Based on
the predicted UE position, the channel gain information can be
identiп¬Ғed by the RT mechanism. The channel gain prediction
procedure is summarized as in Algorithm 2. Given the channel
gain in sub-TW нң…, the optimization problem in sub-TW нң…is
revised from problem (P2) as
(P2)нң…:
max
н‘·B
нң…,н‘·S
нң…,нқҖB
нң…,нқҖS
нң…,нқҒBнң…,нқҒSнң…,н’Ӯнң…,н’ғнң…
нңҢ
н‘Ғpred
TS
ЛңнңҶнң…вҲ’1 вҲ’нңҢ
н‘Ғpred
TS
Лңн‘“CC,(н‘–) (н‘·B
нң…, н‘·S
нң…)
s.t. constraints ( Лңнҗ¶1)нң…вҲ’( Лңнҗ¶12)нң…,
wherein the added low sufп¬Ғx (В·)нң…in the constraint and variable
notations indicates the adaptation of TS range which is limited
within sub-TW нң…. For simpliп¬Ғcation, we assume that sub-
TW size н‘Ғpred
TS
is equal to integer times н‘ҒQoS
TS . To obtain the
solution for problem (P0), channel gain prediction is executed
and problem (P2)нң…is solved for each sub-TW. Particularly,
at the end of sub-TW (нң…вҲ’1), channel gain for sub-TW
нң…is predicted and problem (P2)нң…is solved. The proposed
prediction-based algorithm, so-called prediction-based time-
window (PTW) algorithm, is described in Algorithm 3. Its
convergence is discussed in the following proposition.
Proposition 5. Algorithm 3 is guaranteed for convergence to
a local optimal solution of problem (P0).
Proof: To proof the convergence of Algorithm 3, one
needs to proof the independence among problems (P2)нң…
of different sub-TWs and the convergence in solving each
problem (P2)нң…. Let О©нң…= (н‘·B
нң…, н‘·S
нң…, нқҖB
нң…, нқҖS
нң…, нқҒB
нң…, нқҒS
нң…, н’Ӯнң…, н’ғнң…) be
the variable tuple of problem (P2)нң…. It can be seen that there
is no coupling between variable tuples of problems (P2)нң…and
(P2)нң…вҖІ,вҲҖнң…вү нң…вҖІ, i.e., О©нң…вҲ©О©нң…вҖІ = вҲ…. Therefore, problem (P2)нң…
for each sub-TW нң…can be solved independently. In addition,
problem (P2)нң…has the same structure with problem (P2).
Hence, solving each problem (P2)нң…in steps 4 of Algorithm 3
is guaranteed to converge, and the aggregated solution for all
sub-TWs is the local optimal solution of problem (P0) which
can be proved similarly to the proof of Proposition 4.
Remark 1. The proposed FTW and PTW algorithms are
designed based on the average joint max-sum-rate and min-
CC-number problems (P2) and (P2)нң…, however, they can
easily adapt to the average joint max-sum-rate and min-CC-
number objective with simple modiп¬Ғcations. Particularly, let
нңҶmin
н‘Ў
be the slack variable indicating the minimum UE rate at
TS н‘Ў. Indeed, by the following modiп¬Ғcations:
вҖў replace ЛңнңҶby нңҶmin
н‘Ў
in the objective function,
вҖў replace the rate requirement ВҜн‘…н‘ҳby нңҶmin
н‘Ў
in ( Лңнҗ¶6) and
( Лңнҗ¶6)нң…of problems (P2) and (P2)нң…, respectively,
the proposed FTW and PTW algorithms can be directly
applied to solve average joint max-min-rate and min-CC-
number problems.
D. Prediction-based Algorithm Implementation
To clarify the practical implementation, Fig. 5 depicts
the update and execution procedures of the prediction-based
algorithm. In particular, at the end of each sub-TW нң…, UEs
update their velocity, position, and route via the connected
BS/LSat while LSats update their orbit information to the
CNC. Subsequently, the CNC executes Algorithm 2 to predict
the channel gain for the next sub-TW (нң…+1). Based on which
the CNC executes problem (P2)нң…+1 to п¬Ғnd the solution for the
next sub-TW (нң…+ 1). Since the update and execution proce-
dures occur only once in each sub-TW, this approach reduces
the signaling amount between nodes and the computation
frequency at CNC. Additionally, depending on the stability of
satellite orbits, LSats can update information less frequently
instead of at every sub-TW which further reduces the data
exchange frequency over the long propagation link between
LSats and ground segments. Furthermore, the implementation
analysis with the speciп¬Ғc parameter setting is provided in the
simulation results section.
Remark 2. Thanks to updating the UE route at each sub-
TW, the PTW algorithm can adapt to the changes of the UE
route in practice which avoids the outdated route information
provided at the beginning time.
Remark 3. In practical implementation, the resource alloca-
tion task should be completed within a п¬Ғnite time horizon,
which may vary depending on the practical systems. For
simpliп¬Ғcation, we assume that the computation capacity of
the CNC is adequate and the time horizon for the resource
allocation task is extensive enough.


--- Page 9 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
9
Algorithm 2 CHANNEL GAIN PREDICTION
1: Input: Satellite orbit, BSвҖҷs positions, 3D map data, UEвҖҷs routes, sub-TW
index нң…, and UEвҖҷs velocity and position in the previous sub-TWs.
2: Predict ЛҶн‘Јн‘ҳ,н‘Ўand ЛҶн‘‘н‘ҳ,н‘Ўof UEs in sub-TW нң…by (28) and (29).
3: Identify UE positions from ЛҶн‘‘н‘ҳ,н‘Ўwith respect to given UEвҖҷs routes.
4: Identify channel gain from UE position by RT mechanism.
5: Output: The channel gain information of UEs in sub-TW нң….
Algorithm 3 PTW ALGORITHM FOR SOLVING PROBLEM (P0)
1: Set нң…= 1.
2: while нң…вүӨн‘Ғpred
TW
do
3:
Predict the channel gain in sub-TW нң…by Algorithm 2.
4:
Solve problem (P2)нң…by Algorithm 1 with adaptation for sub-TW нң….
5:
Set нң…= нң…+ 1.
6: end while
7: Recovery UA solutions нң¶and нң·by (26).
8: Output: The solution (нң¶вҳ…, нң·вҳ…, н‘·B,вҳ…, н‘·S,вҳ…).
IV. BENCHMARKS AND COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS
A. Greedy Algorithm
In this benchmark algorithm, the UA is selected based on
the channel gain while the transmit power of each BS/LSat is
allocated uniformly. For BS-UE connection, at each TS, the
BSn вҲ’UEk link is chosen based on the descending channel
gain order if: 1) UEk is still not connected to any BSs
to ensure (нҗ¶1) and 2) the total number of connected and
selected UEs at BSn is still less than ОЁB
н‘ӣto ensure (нҗ¶2). The
same procedure is conducted for the LSat-SUE connection
such that constraints (нҗ¶3) and (нҗ¶4) are satisп¬Ғed. Since only
the channel gain-based UA is considered, the QoS constraint
(нҗ¶6) is not guaranteed. The Greedy Algorithm is described in
Algorithm 4. For convenience, the MATLAB notation is used
to perform the matrix index.
B. Other Benchmark Algorithm
For comparison purposes, we introduce a benchmark al-
gorithm based on our previous work [1]. In [1], we studied
a similar system model to that in this work wherein the
UA is optimized with a п¬Ғxed transmit power. However, in
[1] the objective is only SR maximization. To adapt the
algorithm proposed in [1] to this work, we aim to modify
this algorithm as follows. To consider the multi-objective in
problem (P0), the CC minimization objective н‘“CC(нң¶, нң·) is
added, wherein multi-objective factors нңҢand (1вҲ’нңҢ) are added
into SR and CC objectives. Subsequently, proposition 2 is
applied to н‘“CC(нң¶, нң·), where components |нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’нӣјн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’1| and
|нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’нӣҪн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’1| are convexiп¬Ғed similar to the procedure ap-
plied to |вҲҘн‘·B
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҘ0вҲ’вҲҘн‘·B
н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’1вҲҘ0| and |вҲҘн‘·S
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲҘ0вҲ’вҲҘн‘·S
н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвҲ’1вҲҘ0|,
respectively. Additionally, the transmit power at each BS/LSat
is п¬Ғxed and uniformly allocated. This modiп¬Ғed algorithm is
named the full-time-window UA (FWUA) algorithm.
C. Complexity Analysis
This section analyzes the complexity of the proposed al-
gorithms and benchmark algorithms regarding big-O notation.
One notes that FTW, PTW, and FWUA algorithms involve
solving convex problems. Assuming that the interior method
is used to solve the convex problems, the complexity in terms
of big-O is O(н‘җ1/2(н‘җ+ н‘Ј)н‘Ј2) where н‘җand н‘Јare the numbers
of inequality constraints and variables, respectively [39].
Algorithm 4 GREEDY ALGORITHM
1: Input: Channel gain h = [в„Һн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў) and g = [н‘”н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў]вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘Ў), and
parameters {нң“Bн‘ӣ, нң“Sн‘ҡ, нңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ў, нңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў}вҲҖ(н‘ҡ,н‘ӣ,н‘Ў).
2: Initialize: Zero matrices нң¶and нң·
3: for Each TS н‘Ўdo
4:
while h(:, :, н‘Ў) вү 0 do
5:
Find index ( ЛҶн‘ӣ, ЛҶн‘ҳ) satisfying h( ЛҶн‘ӣ, ЛҶн‘ҳ, н‘Ў) = max(h(:, :, н‘Ў)).
6:
if ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ҳнӣјЛҶн‘ӣ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүӨнң“B
ЛҶн‘ӣвҲ’нңӮB
ЛҶн‘ӣ,н‘Ў
7:
then Update нӣјЛҶн‘ӣ, ЛҶн‘ҳ,н‘Ў= 1 and h(:, ЛҶн‘ҳ, н‘Ў) = 0
8:
else Update h( ЛҶн‘ӣ, :, н‘Ў) := 0
9:
end while
10:
while g(:, :, н‘Ў) вү 0 do
11:
Find index ( ЛҶн‘ҡ, ЛҶн‘ҳ) satisfying g( ЛҶн‘ҡ, ЛҶн‘ҳ, н‘Ў) = max(g(:, :, н‘Ў)).
12:
if ГҚ
вҲҖн‘ҳнӣҪЛҶн‘ҡ,н‘ҳ,н‘ЎвүӨнң“S
ЛҶн‘ҡвҲ’нңӮS
ЛҶн‘ҡ,н‘Ў
13:
then Update нӣҪЛҶн‘ҡ, ЛҶн‘ҳ,н‘Ў= 1 and g(:, ЛҶн‘ҳ, н‘Ў) = 0
14:
else Update g( ЛҶн‘ҡ, :, н‘Ў) := 0
15:
end while
16: end for
17: Output: UA solution нң¶, нң·.
1) FTW Algorithm: As described in Algorithm 1, the FTW
Algorithm includes one loop in Steps 2-6, wherein problem
(P2) is solved iteratively. Since problem (P2) consists of
н‘ЈFTW = 3н‘Ғнҗҫн‘ҒTS + 3н‘Җнҗҫн‘ҒTS + н‘Ғн‘ҒTS + н‘Җн‘ҒTS variables and
н‘җFTW = 4н‘Ғнҗҫн‘ҒTS + 4н‘Җнҗҫн‘ҒTS + 2н‘Ғн‘ҒTS + 2н‘Җн‘ҒTS + 5нҗҫн‘ҒTS +
нҗҫн‘ҒTS/н‘ҒQoS
TS
inequality constraints, the complexity of the
FTW Algorithm can be expressed as
н‘ӢFTW = O(н‘Ғiterн‘җ1/2
FTW(н‘җFTW + н‘ЈFTW)н‘Ј3
FTW),
(30)
wherein н‘Ғiter is the required number iteration for convergence.
2) PTW Algorithm: Regarding Algorithm 3, the PTW Al-
gorithm consists of two loops: the outer loop corresponds
to the number of sub-TWs н‘Ғpred
TW
and the inner loop relates
to solving problem (P2)нң…iteratively. Since problem (P2)нң…
consists of н‘ЈPTW = 3н‘Ғнҗҫн‘Ғpred
TS +3н‘Җнҗҫн‘Ғpred
TS +н‘Ғн‘Ғpred
TS +н‘Җн‘Ғpred
TS
variables and н‘җPTW = 4н‘Ғнҗҫн‘Ғpred
TS
+ 4н‘Җнҗҫн‘Ғpred
TS
+ 2н‘Ғн‘Ғpred
TS
+
2н‘Җн‘Ғpred
TS +5нҗҫн‘Ғpred
TS +нҗҫн‘Ғpred
TS /н‘ҒQoS
TS
inequality constraints, the
complexity of the PTW Algorithm can be expressed as
н‘ӢPTW = O(н‘Ғiterн‘Ғpred
TW н‘җ1/2
PTW(н‘җPTW + н‘ЈPTW)н‘Ј3
PTW).
(31)
3) Greedy Algorithm: Regarding Algorithm 4, the Greedy
Algorithm consists of one outer-loop with н‘ҒTS loops and two
inner-loops for BS-UE and LSat-UA, respectively. In the п¬Ғrst
inner-loop in Step 4-8, one needs to execute нҗҫtimes until
h(:, :, н‘Ў) = 0 in worst case, wherein п¬Ғnding and updating in
Step 5-8 demand a complexity of O(н‘Ғнҗҫ+ н‘Ғ) for each time.
The computation for each time in the second inner loop is
identiп¬Ғed similarly. In summary, the complexity of the Greedy
Algorithm can be given as
н‘ӢGre.Alg. = O(н‘ҒTS(н‘Ғнҗҫ2 + н‘Ғнҗҫ+ н‘Җнҗҫ2 + н‘Җнҗҫ)).
(32)
4) FWUA Algorithm: In the FWUA Algorithm, a convex
problem for UA is solved iteratively until convergence. The
convex problem associated with the FTWUA Algorithm con-
sists of н‘ЈFWUA = 3н‘Ғнҗҫн‘ҒTS+3н‘Җнҗҫн‘ҒTS+н‘Ғн‘ҒTS+н‘Җн‘ҒTS variables
and н‘җFWUA = 5н‘Ғнҗҫн‘ҒTS +5н‘Җнҗҫн‘ҒTS + н‘Ғн‘ҒTS + н‘Җн‘ҒTS +5нҗҫн‘ҒTS +
нҗҫн‘ҒTS/н‘ҒQoS
TS
inequality constraints. Therefore, the complexity
of the FTWUA Algorithm can be expressed as
н‘ӢFWUA = O(н‘Ғiterн‘җ1/2
FWUA(н‘җFWUA + н‘ЈFWUA)н‘Ј3
FWUA).
(33)

V. NUMERICAL RESULTS
A. Simulation Setup
The simulation is conducted in urban environments on an
area in London city limited by latitude and longitude ranges of
[51.5115в—ҰN, 51.5965в—ҰN] and [0.1772в—ҰW, 0.0028в—ҰE], respec-
tively. Subsequently, the map is divided into a grid of segments
where the size of each segment is (0.005в—ҰГ—0.005в—Ұ) in latitude
and longitude, respectively. To evaluate the link budget across
map locations, the receiver grid size in each map segment
is (100 Г— 100) points. It is worth noting that UEs are the
vehicles, thus receiver points are deployed outdoors. Regarding
the transmitters, one assumes that one BS is deployed in each
map segment at the highest building. In addition, one LEO
constellation at 500 km is considered. To reduce the shadow
area caused by high-buildings when LSats at low elevation
angle and improve the coverage, one assumes that there are
2 LSats which can be used in the FoV at each instance time.
Regarding UE mobility, the UEвҖҷs routes are obtained by the
navigator data in the Google Map application. Additionally,
the number of TUEs at BSн‘ӣнңӮB
н‘ӣ,н‘Ўand SUEs at LSatн‘ҡнңӮS
н‘ҡ,н‘Ў
are assumed to be Poisson random variables with means ВҜнңӮB
н‘ӣ
and ВҜнңӮS
н‘ҡ. Furthermore, to consider the heterogeneity of UE
number in the considered area, the means number of TUEs
at BSs ВҜнңӮB
н‘ӣare set proportionally to the population distribution
in the area which is extracted from LondonвҖҷs age data set [40]
and depicted in Fig. 6. Particularly, the population in each
map segment is computed based on the dataset, then ВҜнңӮB
н‘ӣfor
each map segment is scaled proportionally to its population
200
230
260
290
320
350
380
410
440
20
40
60
80
100
Number of BSs
Coverage percentage
BSs and 2 LSats
BSs and 1 LSat
Only BSs
Fig. 8: The coverage percentage (CINR вүҘ3 dB) versus the number
of deployed BSs.

10
so that maxн‘ӣ{ ВҜнңӮB
н‘ӣ} = ВҜнңӮB
max. The key simulation parameters are
described in Table. I wherein parameter set 1 for co-existence
study in 3GPP TR 38-863 is utilized. In practical systems,
if the power п¬Ӯux density limit is explicit, the maximum
LSat transmit power could be adjusted appropriately. The
simulations are carried out on a computer equipped with an
Intel Xeon E5 CPU @ 2.4 GHz and 128 GB RAM, wherein the
iterative algorithms are implemented by using the MATLAB
programming environment, leveraging the CVX modeling
framework and MOSEK solver. For an intuitive view of
complicated environments in urban areas, we п¬Ғrst investigate
the link budget assessment in terms of the BS/LSat-UE linkвҖҷs
CINR with practical data. Subsequently, the numerical results
are carried out to evaluate the performance in terms of SR and
CC number optimization of the considered algorithms.


--- Page 11 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
11
(a) CINR of BS/LSat-UE1 (above) and BS/LSat-UE7 (below) links over TSs.
(b) CINR of BS-UE links.
(c) CINR of LSat-UE links.
Fig. 9: CINR of BS/LSat-UE1 and BS/LSat-UE7 links over TSs corresponding to the CINR heatmap in a small area.
B. Link Budget Simulation Results
This section carries out the simulation to assess the link
budget in terms of the CINR of BS/LSat-UE links in urban
environments. The CINR of links can be calculated as de-
scribed in [41]. Additionally, based on the outcomes of RT
simulation, i.e., propagation loss, phase delay, and AoA and
AoD of rays, the equivalent PL of each link is computed by
(1). To be more intuitive, the RT simulation of the BS-UE
and LSat-UE links is shown in Fig. 7, where the elevation
angle at the receiver points to LSat is 60в—Ұ. This shows the
impact of building and the complicated propagation of rays
from BS/LSat to the receiver points in urban environments,
which is difп¬Ғcult to determine mathematically.
To evaluate the effectiveness of ISTNs in improving cover-
age, Fig. 8 illustrates the percentage of coverage area with
CINR вүҘ3 dB of different deployment options versus the
number of BSs. Obviously, increasing the number of BSs
improves coverage. However, based on the trend, one may
require to deploy a large number of BSs to achieve more than
90% coverage. In contrast, using integrated BSs with 1 or 2
LSats can signiп¬Ғcantly increase the coverage percentage, i.e.,
more than 90% of the considered area. This also demonstrates
the cost-effectiveness of ISTNs in improving coverage and
facilitating seamless connectivity.
Fig. 9 shows the CINR heatmap of BS/LSat-UE links and
the time-varying CINR of two UEs corresponding to their
route in a considered area. Regarding the CINR heatmap,
one can see the heterogeneity of both BS-UE and LSat-UE
links. In particular, the CINR of BS-UE links is improved in
areas with dense buildings and dense BS deployment. This
is due to the blockage of the high and dense building to
the LSat-UE links while the BS-UE links against the impact
of the building by dense deployment of BSs as depicted in
Fig. 7. Additionally, the CINR of LSat-UE links increases
in the area with fewer buildings. Interestingly, despite the
signiп¬Ғcant propagation loss due to the long distance of the
LSat-UE links compared to BS-UE ones, its CINR is higher
than that of the BS-UE link in certain areas. This phenomenon
is due to the impact of the antenna gain pattern at the UE
which is equipped with the patch antenna. In particular, the
elevation angle at the UE towards the LSat can be seen as
constant across the map due to the long UE-LSat distance as
in Fig. 7. This elevation angle is in a range of about 60в—ҰвҲ’90в—Ұ,
which results in an antenna gain at UE of about 7.3 вҲ’12.8
dBi as shown in Fig. 2. However, except for locations near
BSs, the BS-UE links are transmitted almost in the horizontal
plane with a low elevation angle, i.e., about 20в—ҰвҲ’30в—Ұ, leading
to the low antenna gain at UE, i.e., about вҲ’26.3 dBi to
вҲ’12.8 dBi. For instance, at locations wherein the elevation
angles of LSat-UE and BS-UE links are 60в—Ұand 30в—Ұ, the
gap in antenna gain between two links is about 20.1 dBi.
Therefore, in areas where there exists a signiп¬Ғcant antenna
gain disparity, the antenna gain can compensate the distance
gap between the LSat-UE and BS-UE links, allowing the LSat-
UE links to outperform the BS-UE links in terms of CINR.
Additionally, regarding time-varying CINR, one can see the
correlation between the UE routes on the CINR heatmap and
their CINR over TSs. This change in both BS/LSat-UE links
is due to the combination of UE mobility, LSat movement,
and building inп¬Ӯuence. Depending on the UE location, the
quality of BS/LSat-UE links is different, especially the LSat-
UE links which are time-dependence due to LSat movement.
In particular, the CINR can be changed with different positive
magnitudes in a range of about 5вҲ’30 dB or even be negative,
i.e., the link is blocked. This emphasizes the necessity of PA
and UA optimization as formulated in problem (P0).
C. Numerical Results
This section discusses the numerical results of the proposed
and benchmark algorithms in solving the SR-CC problem.
Fig. 10 shows the convergence of the objective function which
consists of the average SR and the CC number of the FTW,
PTW, and FTUA algorithms versus iterations. All considered
algorithms require only a few tens iterations for convergence.
Due to the optimization of only UA, the FWUA algorithm
requires the smallest number of iterations for convergence
among three, about 14 iterations. Additionally, the proposed
FTW and PTW algorithms need a slightly higher number of
iterations, i.e., about 23 and 24 iterations, respectively. In
practical implementation, if the running time of the proposed
algorithms is too long, one can stop the execution before
convergence to obtain a good enough solution.

FWUA Alg.
Fig. 10: Convergence of the objective function.

0.4
0.8
1.2
1.6
2
2.4
2.8
0
5

15
20
25
ПҒ = 0.1
ПҒ = 0.3
ПҒ = 0.5
ПҒ = 0.7
ПҒ = 0.9
Average number of CCs
Average sum-rate [bps/Hz]
FTW Alg.
PTW Alg.
FWUA Alg.
Fig. 11: Pareto front of the average SR and CC number vs multi-
objective factor нңҢ.
Fig. 11 depicts the Pareto curves of the average SR and
CC number per TS of the considered algorithms versus multi-
objective factor нңҢ. One can see that the Pareto curves of the
two proposed algorithms are close and outperform that of the
FWUA algorithm in terms of the SR and the CC number.
Particularly, at нңҢ= 0.9, нңҢ= 0.7, and нңҢ= 0.5, both SR and the
CC number results provided by the two proposed algorithms
are better than those by the FWUA algorithm. At нңҢ= 0.3
and нңҢ= 0.1, the FWUA algorithm can provide a better SR
compared to the two proposed algorithms, however, the CC
number outcome is worse. Obviously, a higher нңҢincreases the
priority of SR maximization and decreases that of CC number
minimization. However, for the FTW and PTW algorithms, the
Pareto curve increases quickly from нңҢ= 0.1 to нңҢ= 0.7, i.e., SR
rapidly raises and the CC number slowly increases, whereas
it slowly increases from нңҢ= 0.7 to нңҢ= 0.9. Particularly, SR
increases about 10.6, 5.9, and 2.6 bps/Hz and the average CC
number increases about 0.15, 0.23, and 0.35 when increasing
нңҢ= 0.1 вҶ’0.3, нңҢ= 0.3 вҶ’0.5, and нңҢ= 0.5 вҶ’0.7, whereas
the average SR and the CC number increase about 2.27 bps/Hz
and 0.6 when increasing нңҢ= 0.7 вҶ’0.9, respectively. Based
on this observation, нңҢ= 0.7 is selected to balance between the
SR and CC number.
Fig. 12 shows the statistic channel prediction error in terms
of the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). Obviously, a
larger prediction window leads to a higher channel prediction
error. Particularly, the MAPE values are about 0.07 and 0.14
at н‘Ғpred
TS
= 60 TSs and н‘Ғpred
TS
= 180 TSs, respectively. For
more information on the impact of prediction window size,
Fig. 13 illustrates the performance of the PTW Algorithm in

90
120
150
180

PTW Alg.
Fig. 12: Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of Alg. 2 vs.
prediction sub-TW size.

Size of the prediction window, N pred
TS
Average sum-rate [bps/Hz]
FTW Alg.
PTW Alg.
FWUA Alg.
60
90
120
150
180
0.6
0.8
1
1.2
1.4
1.6
1.8
2
Average number of CCs
Fig. 13: Sum-rate and CC number of Alg. 3 vs. prediction sub-TW
size.
terms of the average SR and the CC number with the different
sizes of prediction sub-TW н‘Ғpred
TS
and those of the FTW and
FWUA algorithms as the baselines. Regarding the CC, since
the sub-TW is optimized independently, a longer prediction
window size leads to a lower number of sub-TW, thus the CC
number decreases. However, a larger window size results in
less accuracy in channel gain prediction as MAPE increases,
leading to degradation of SR performance. Additionally, the
UA decision is made based on the predicted channel, the
higher prediction error may lead to more unsuitable UA
decisions with the actual channel, which also contributes to
the SR degradation. For instance, the SR degradation is about
2 bps/Hz and the average number of CCs decreases about 0.15
when raising н‘Ғpred
TS
from 60 to 180. This can be considered
as the trade-off between SR and CC performances. However,
these performances can be balanced and comparable with
those of the FTW Algorithm by choosing an appropriate
prediction sub-TW size.
Fig. 14 depicts the impact of the BSвҖҷs power budget on the
average SR and number of CCs. Regarding the CC results,
one can see that the change in BSвҖҷs power budget does not
signiп¬Ғcantly affect the average number of CCs. However, two
proposed FTW and PTW Algorithms outperform the two other
benchmarks in terms of average CC number. In particular, the
average CC number outcome of the two proposed algorithms


--- Page 13 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
13
Fig. 14: Sum-rate and CC number vs. BS maximum transmit power.
is about 0.65 whereas those of the FWUA and Greedy Algo-
rithms are about 1.9 and 8.7, respectively. Regarding the SR,
the trends of the FWUA and Greedy Algorithms п¬Ғrst decrease
and then increase as the BSвҖҷs power budget increases. This
phenomenon arises because interference from BSs to LSat-
UE links increases with the BSвҖҷs power budget, becoming
signiп¬Ғcant at particular BS power levels. Subsequently, at a
sufп¬Ғciently high BS power, the SR offered by BS-UE links
dominates that by LSat-UE links, which leads to an increase
in system SR. Additionally, increasing the BSвҖҷs power budget
can improve the BS-UE links. This is shown by the increase
in the ratio of the SR contributed by BSs with the BSвҖҷs power
budget. Regarding the two proposed algorithms, the ratio is
about 22% at н‘ғB,max = 34 dBm and 43% at н‘ғB,max = 46 dBm.
Besides, due to the п¬Ӯexibility of PA in the FTW and PTW
algorithms, the two proposed algorithms offer a signiп¬Ғcant
SR improvement. Especially, the SR gap between the two
lines is not signiп¬Ғcant, i.e., about 0.5 bps/Hz. For instance,
SR outcomes of the FTW and PTW algorithms are about 19.1
and 18.6 bps/Hz at н‘ғB,max = 40 dBm, respectively.
For more insight into the impact of BSвҖҷs power budget,
Fig. 15 displays the average number of UEs served by BSs and
LSats with the different н‘ғB,max. Regarding the two proposed
algorithms, one can see that the average number of UEs served
in BSs increases as н‘ғB,max increases. For instance, that is about
3.8 and 4.7 UEs at н‘ғB,max = 34 dBm and н‘ғB,max = 46 dBm,
respectively. Additionally, that number at LSats decreases
as н‘ғBS,max increases, which is about 8.1 and 7.3 UEs at
н‘ғB,max = 34 dBm and н‘ғB,max = 46 dBm, respectively. This
indicates that UEs priories BSsвҖҷs connections to achieve a
higher SR as the BSвҖҷs power budget increases. Furthermore,
the average numbers of served UEs at BSs and LSats provided
by the proposed FTW and PTW Algorithms are less than that
by the greedy algorithm. This emphasizes the effectiveness of
the proposed algorithms wherein they require less number of
connections while providing better SR compared to the two
benchmark ones, i.e., the FWUA and greedy algorithms.
Fig. 16 shows the average SR and number of CCs versus
the LSatвҖҷs power budget. Regarding the CC number, this is
similar to that in Fig. 14, wherein its change is not signiп¬Ғcant
as different н‘ғS,max. Regarding the SR performance, the trends
of the four considered algorithms are similar. In particular, as
34
36
38
40
42
44
46
4
5

Maximum transmit power at the BS, P B,max [dBm]
Average #UEs served by BSs
FTW Alg.

34
36

7

10

12
Average #UEs served by LSats
Fig. 15: Served-UE number vs BS maximum transmit power.
Fig. 16: Sum-rate and the number of CCs versus the maximum
transmit power at the LSat.
the power budget at LSats increases, the SR п¬Ғrst drops due to
the increase of interference from LSats to BS-UE links. Subse-
quently, the SR rises at a sufп¬Ғciently high LSat power budget
due to the domination quality of LSat-UE links compared to
that of BS-UE links. For instance, at н‘ғS,max = (6, 12, 16)
dBW, the SR provided by the FWT and PTW Algorithms is
approximately (18.8, 18.4, 20.4) bps/Hz and (18.4, 17.9, 19.8)
bps/Hz, respectively. Furthermore, the SR contribution ratio
of LSats increases with the LSatвҖҷs power budget due to the
LSat-UE linkвҖҷs improvement. Particularly, regarding the two
proposed algorithms, LSats contributes about 24% and 74%
of SR at н‘ғS,max = 6 dBW and н‘ғS,max = 16 dBW, respectively.
Compared to SR at different н‘ғBS,max in Fig. 14, the SR
degradation in this п¬Ғgure shows a more critical impact of
interference from LSats to BS-UE links, even with PA of the
two proposed algorithms. This is explained by the impact of
interference from LSats on BS-UE links in a vast area, whereas
BSs cause interference on LSat-UE links locally due to the
isolation by high buildings.
For more insight into the impact of LSatвҖҷs power budget,
Fig. 17 shows the average number of served UEs at BSs and at
LSats with the different cases of н‘ғS,max. In the two proposed
algorithms, the UEs reduce the connections to the BSs and
increase those at LSats as the maximum LSat power increases
since the SR offered by LSats is improved. Particularly, the
average numbers of UEs served at BSs and LSats are about 5.7

Fig. 18: Sum-rate and CC number versus the minimum rate require-
ment.
and 6.3 at н‘ғS,max = 6 dBW, and about 4.2 and 7.8 at н‘ғS,max =
16 dBW. Furthermore, compared to the benchmark algorithms,
this п¬Ғgure again shows the effectiveness of the two proposed
algorithms in terms of SR and connection number wherein
their solutions have similar or fewer numbers of connections
but better SR performance.
Subsequently, Fig. 18 illustrates the average SR and the
number of CCs versus the different rate requirement
ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ.
Regarding the FWUA algorithm, the SR performance changes
insigniп¬Ғcantly with different rate requirements. However, to
satisfy the QoS constraint, the number of CCs increases
as
ВҜн‘…н‘ҳincreases. For the two proposed algorithms, as
ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ
increases, the SR outcomes decrease whereas the numbers
of CCs increase. One can see that the increase in the rate
requirement leads to a smaller feasible set of the SR-CC
problem, which results in degradation in the SR and CC
performance. Particularly, at ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.2 bps/Hz, the average
SR results of the FTW and PTW algorithms are about 20.12
bps/Hz and 19.45 bps/Hz, and the CC number is about 0.9; at
ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 1 bps/Hz, the those SR results are about 18.8 bps/Hz and
18.1 bps/Hz, and the CC number is about 1.7, respectively.
However, at all considered rate requirement levels, the two
proposed algorithms always outperform the FWUA and greedy
algorithms in terms of SR and the CC-number.
For more information on the impact of QoS requirements,
Fig. 19 shows the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of
the achievable rate with rate requirement ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0 bps/Hz and

1
Rate requirement
ВҜRk = 0.6 bps/Hz
FWT Alg.
PWT Alg.
FWUA Alg.
Fig. 19: The cumulative distribution function of the achievable rate
at the minimum rate requirements of ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0 bps/Hz and ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6
bps/Hz.
ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6 bps/Hz. Regarding the FWUA algorithm, the CDF
of achievable rates at ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0 bps/Hz and ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6 bps/Hz
are similar, which further clariп¬Ғes the near constancy of SR
in Fig. 18. In contrast, the CDFs of the rate outcomes of the
FTW and PTW algorithms change signiп¬Ғcantly at ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0
bps/Hz and ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6 bps/Hz. Particularly, compared to the
CDFs at ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0 bps/Hz, those at ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6 bps/Hz are forced
to satisfy the minimum rate requirement. However, due to the
channel prediction error in the PWT algorithm, one can see
the degradation of the achievable rate, especially at lower than
ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6 bps/Hz. However, the probabilities of rate outcomes
of the FTW and PTW algorithms lower than ВҜн‘…н‘ҳ= 0.6 bps/Hz
are relatively small, i.e., about 0.03 and 0.2, respectively.
To assess computational efп¬Ғciency, the average running time
of the PTW algorithm with varying prediction window sizes
н‘Ғpred
TS
and those of the benchmark, i.e., the FTW and FTUA
algorithms, are shown in Fig. 20. Since the FTW and FTUA
algorithms solve the problem over the entire time window,
they incur an extremely high time for execution, i.e., about
632.3 and 512.4 minutes. In contrast, the PTW algorithm
exhibits much lower running time. While its running time
increases slightly with larger prediction window size, due to
the increasing size of problem (P2)нң…, it remains substantially
more efп¬Ғcient than the FTW and FTUA ones. Speciп¬Ғcally, for
н‘Ғpred
TS
= (60, 90, 120, 150, 180) TSs, the average running time
of the PTW algorithm is approximately 6.2, 8.4, 11.1, 14.2,
and 18.31 minutes, respectively. Notably, the proposed PTW
algorithm can achieve performance comparable to the FTW
algorithm and even outperforms the FTUA one in terms of
max-SR and min-CC-number, while requiring only a fraction
of the running time, i.e, approximately 1% вҲ’3.5% of the
benchmarksвҖҷ running time.
D. Practical Implementation Analysis
According to the simulation setting, assuming that the
CNC is deployed at the center of the examined area, the
distance between the CNC and BSs is about 7.8 km leading
to the propagation time between CNC and BSs of н‘ҮCNC,BS вүӨ
0.03 Вөs. Besides, due to the satellite orbit altitude of 500 km,
the propagation time between the CNC and LSats is about


--- Page 15 ---
ACCEPTED FOR PUBLICATION IN IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMMUNICATIONS
15
60
90
120
150
180
0
100
200
300
400
500
600
Size of the prediction window, N pred
TS
Average running time (min)
FTW Alg.
PTW Alg.
FTUA Alg.
60
90
120
150
180
5
10
15
Fig. 20: Average running time of examined iterative algorithms.
BSs
LSats
CNC
UEs
Fig. 21: Propagation and signaling time consumption.
н‘ҮCNC,LSat вүҲ1.7 ms. The time consumption due to propagation
and signaling is depicted in Fig. 21. In the best case where
UEs can update their status via BSs, this time consumption
is about н‘Үbest = 2 Г— н‘ҮCNC,LSat = 3.4 ms, while in the worst
case where existing UEs which need to update their status
via LSats this time is about н‘Үworst = 3 Г— н‘ҮCNC,LSat = 5.1 ms.
Additionally, for TS duration of н‘ҮS = 0.5 s and the sub-TW
size of н‘Ғpred
TS
= 90 TSs as in simulation setting, the sub-
TW duration is н‘ҮsubвҲ’TW = 45 s. One can see that the time
consumption for propagation and signaling in the worst case
is negligible compared to sub-TW duration. Hence, this further
shows the practicality of the proposed PTW algorithm.
Besides, the segmentation of the map in the considered
area can reduce the complexity of the prediction mechanism
which facilitates scalability. Particularly, based on the outcome
of the UE position prediction step, for each UE, one can
determine the map segment corresponding to its position in a
sub-TW. Based on which the RT mechanism can execute for
the identiп¬Ғed map segment rather than the entire considered
map. Furthermore, these steps can be executed in a parallel
manner for UEs which further facilitates scalability.
VI. CONCLUSIONS
This work studied the joint UA and PA for the practical
co-primary spectrum sharing ISTNs in urban environments.
Subsequently, the multi-objective of SR maximization and the
number of CCs minimization under the resource limitation
constraints is formulated. To solve this problem, we proposed
two algorithms: 1) FTW: an iterative algorithm for the entire
time-window and 2) PTW: a prediction-based algorithm for
the implementation purpose that sequentially predicts the
channel gain and solves the problem for sub-time-windows.
For practice evaluation, simulation with the actual 3D map
and UE mobility is performed. The simulation results provide
insight into the link budget assessment in urban environments.
Additionally, numerical results show the effectiveness of our
two proposed algorithms compared to the greedy algorithm
and the benchmark one (FTUA) in terms of both the system
SR and the CC number.
The approach of using RT, 3D map, and realistic mobility
can be used as a framework for future works. Moreover, this
study provides opportunities for further extensions, particu-
larly in improving the channel model as well as addressing
prediction error and intra-system-interference issues. Speciп¬Ғ-
cally, for practical applications, random impacts in the actual
environment can be characterised in the channel model by
incorporating corresponding statistical components. Besides,
the prediction mechanism in the proposed PTW algorithm
could be improved to reduce prediction error and SR degra-
dation. For more in-depth analysis, the potential intra-system-
interference issues should be considered in future research.
APPENDIX A
PROOF OF PROPOSITION 2
Consider function н‘“abs(н‘Ҙ, н‘Ұ)
=
|вҲҘн‘ҘвҲҘ0 вҲ’вҲҘн‘ҰвҲҘ0|,

0 which has the form of components in the CC-number
function.
н‘“abs(н‘Ҙ, н‘Ұ) can be represented as
н‘“abs(н‘Ҙ, н‘Ұ)

н‘ўlow вүӨвҲҘн‘ҘвҲҘ0, н‘ўlow вүӨвҲҘн‘ҰвҲҘ0,

PROOF OF PROPOSITION 3

н‘Ҙ

 ln(н‘Ҙ+ н‘Ұ+ н‘җ) вүҘн‘§+ н‘ў,

(39a)

(39b)
